description.narrative: The Project will strengthen the resilience of vulnerable smallholder farmers in the country’s Dry Zone, particularly women, who are facing increasing risks of rising temperatures, erratic rainfall, and extreme events attributable to climate change.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

description.narrative:
SDG 16 Indicator - 3.3.1.1. Percentage of community members (disaggregated by sex) satisfied with local authorities’ sub-projects (measured by provinces) (Numerator: # of people satisfied; Denominator: # of people approached for services)
SDG 16 Indicator - 3.3.1.2: Number of By-laws adopted/amended to provide for increased efficiency of service delivery. (CDLG 1.1.5)

title.narrative: % of LAs with digitized systems for enhanced accountability, efficient service delivery and enhanced management of internal control processes

description.narrative:
LAs refers to the target LAs in the Province. Digitized systems - At least one function either at enhancing internal control processes or at service delivery established with the support of CDLG project
Enhanced Accountability - Enhanced accountability refers to measures implemented to improve the level of transparency and responsibility of individuals, organizations, or systems. Any systems that facilitate access to the right holders/service recipients to provide their concerns/feedback/proposals and for duty bearers to provide their feedback

title.narrative: Extent to which Local organizations including LAs and CCs enhanced participation and accountability in local governance

description.narrative:
Numerator – No of LAs in the target provinces that are using introduced tools (for accountability) Denominator – No of LAs in the targeted provinces Tools – that are developed to increase the transparency and accountability.
Definition of tools Controls – Procurement, budget and expenditure details
Other – CAT 2020, Web pages, committee S
For CDLG at least one tool under each category (controls and other)

title.narrative: No of LA enhanced their public communication portals (online/social media)

description.narrative: This indicator is set to measure the LAs that have established at least one medium to communicate with the public on the activities of LAs including procurement notices, and other service related notifications., with the support of CDLG

title.narrative: No of SI projects implemented (disag ) of which no of lessons/best practices considered for annual planning and budgeting (disaggregated by PS/MC/UC).

description.narrative: No of LAs (disaggregated by PS/UC/MC) designed and implemented social innovation projects Social Innovation – an innovative solution to a local issue identified participatorily.

title.narrative: No of LAs adopted resilient measures (climate adaptation, innovation, crisis management)( DRR plans in their annual planning and budgeting, SWM, etc.)

description.narrative: This indicator measures the number of local authorities (LAs) that have adopted with the support of CDLG increased resilient measures for climate adaptation, innovation, crisis management, disaster risk reduction (DRR) plans in their annual planning and budgeting, solid waste management (SWM), and other related activities.

title.narrative: No of LAs reporting average increase in revenue of LAs (Revenue includes all sources of revenue at the disposal of local government, including property tax, user charges, fees and fines and from alternative sources like investments/PPP and savings from digitization of systems)

description.narrative: The no of Las reporting of average increase in revenue of local governments with the support of CDLG measures the change in the total revenue earned by local governments in a given period compared to the revenue earned in a previous period.

title.narrative: No of LAs incorporated lessons and best practices in their annual plans gained from the exchange visits

description.narrative: This indicator measures the number of local authorities that have incorporated lessons and best practices learned from exchange visits supported by CDLG into their annual plans.

title.narrative: No and types of Provincial and Central institutions were supported to develop their capacity to enhance the service delivery of LAs

description.narrative: This indicator measures the number and types of provincial and central institutions that have received support and training by CDLG to develop their capacity to enhance service delivery of local governments. Capacity building activities may include training on technical skills, organizational development, leadership and management, and other areas necessary to improve the delivery of services to citizens..

title.narrative: No of joint initiatives planned and implemented by devolved and deconcentrated structures at sub0national level

description.narrative: This indicator measures the extent of collaboration and partnership between devolved and deconcentrated structures, including local governments and other relevant stakeholders at the sub-national level in planning and implementing joint initiatives aimed at achieving development objectives, with the support of CDLG

title.narrative: No of by-laws, regulations, guidelines, directives and internal policies developed. ((3c.1 qualified/approved by the national and provincial structures (gender, revenue, environment, disability inclusion etc.))

description.narrative: This indicator refers to the total number of legislative and policy documents created and adopted by LAs to regulate local governance, service delivery, and overall municipal operations. These may include by-laws, regulations, guidelines, directives, and internal policies that help to determine how services are provided to citizens, how municipal resources are managed, and how local decision-making is carried out.

title.narrative: % of local organizations including LAs, CCs have increased service delivery through strengthened internal management (planning and budgeting, administration, Regulatory services, Finance, Procurement)

description.narrative: Strengthened Internal Management Strengthened Internal Management in local governments refers to the implementation of policies, procedures, and processes designed to improve the efficiency, accountability, and transparency of local government operations. This includes the establishment of mechanisms for effective financial management, human resource management, risk management, and performance management. Strengthened internal management also involves the development and implementation of appropriate governance structures and controls to prevent corruption, ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations, and enhance service delivery to communities. Ultimately, it seeks to enhance the operational capacity and effectiveness of local governments to better serve the needs of their constituents.

title.narrative: No of LAs improved their financial practices resulting in improved audit opinions

description.narrative: This indicator measures the number of local authorities that have improved their financial practices with the support of CDLG, resulting in improved audit opinions. Improved financial practices may include better compliance with financial regulations, effective budgeting, improved financial reporting, and increased financial transparency.

title.narrative: % of LAs following new procurement guidelines

description.narrative: This indicator measures the extent to which local governments have prepared procurement plans that adhere to the guidelines set out by the local government.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

Output 2: Improved linkages between government and civil society to inform inclusive frameworks that respond to the challenges of violent extremism 2.1. Partnership mapping and gap analysis with key stakeholders on the PVE related legislative and policy environment in Sri Lanka conducted 2.2 # of multi-stakeholder dialogues conducted with government, civil society (particularly with women, youth and religious groups), and PVE experts to develop inclusive recommendations for PVE policy and frameworks
Output 3: Increased knowledge and tools available to communities to counter violent extremism, hate speech and intolerance 3.1 Periodic political economy scoping and gender analysis assessments carried out 3.2 Periodic community assessments to identify areas of tension and gaps in local governance and of existing civil society actors and entry points conducted

Output 1: Data and Trend Analysis is available to stakeholders to identify early signs of radicalisation and violent extremism 1.1: 1 (Working group set in participation of 09 civil society organizations) 1.2: 09 grants provided for CSOs. to support working group activities 1.4: 1 (Operationalized UNDP Crisis Risk Dashboard for Sri Lanka in collaboration with UNDP Crisis Bureau and the RCO)
Output 2: Improved linkages between government and civil society to inform inclusive frameworks that respond to the challenges of violent extremism 2.1: 1 (Sri Lanka’s first primary data-based research into the drivers of violent extremism and spread of hate speech in Sri Lanka. The findings shared for UN country conflict analysis) 2.2: Platforms for community and inter-cultural dialogs established- 60 online dialogs/ 3 community radio dialogs and 15 face to face dialogs
Output 3: Increased knowledge and tools available to communities to counter violent extremism, hate speech and intolerance 3.1: 1 (Launched a series of Political Economy and Gender Analysis of current PVE interventions and online and offline trends in Sri Lanka 3.2: 1 (A Household survey conducted to assess the socio-economic and social cohesion impact of Covid-19 4/6 survey cycles (representing 2000 households) completed. The findings contributed to the UN advisory paper to the GoSL)

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

title.narrative: Status of species diversity in terms of endemic, restricted and threatened faunal and floral species in the target priority sites as measured by key taxonomic groups (flowering plants, dragon flies, butterflies, freshwater fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals

description.narrative: Covers eight taxa, namely Flowering Plants, Dragonflies, Butterflies, Freshwater Fish, Amphibians, Reptiles, Birds and Mammals. Monitored would be actual change in the total number of species, and the number of critical species (endemic, threatened and range restricted).

comment.narrative: Baseline data exists in around 40 estates for various species varies as follows among estates: 58-152 faunal and 32-82 floral species in Nuwara Eliya district; 163-177 faunal species and 87-183 floral species in Matara district; 23-200 faunal species and 25-277 floral species in Kegalle district; and 81-214 faunal species and 177-334 floral species in Rathnapura district.

Validation and collation of existing baselines for estates in an accessible database. Additionally baselines will be established for at least 10 other estates with large forest patches (defined through work under Indicator 2) and monitoring continued on regular basis. Stable or increased species diversity as indicated in baseline values

title.narrative: Number of plantation districts mapped for priority high value forests, conservation value assessed, options for connectivity established and management plans developed for key conservation clusters.

description.narrative: Plantation districts mapped for priority HVCFs, conservation value of these are assessed, options to enhance connectivity are looked into, management plans developed for key conservation clusters

comment.narrative: Forest base maps exists for the districts of Kalutara, Kegalle, Galle, Matara and Ratnapura, while in Nuwara Eliya district RPCs would have information for its individual estates. Therefore during the implementation of the project, plantations in other districts such as Matale, Kandy, Badulla, Kurunegala, Monaragala and Colombo for which GIS based maps are lacking, maps will be developed through the LUPPD.

Forest baseline maps validated through ground truthing and information accessed for the districts where such information is currently unavailable. Based on this exercise, forest extents verified, large and priority conservation patches identified, including options for promoting connectivity and included in database for future actions mapping completed in 5 districts covering around 5,000 hectares

description.narrative:
Outcome 1: Systematic research and evidence generation supporting the countering of online hate speech for effective advocacy and preventive action (UNDP) Indicator 1a: Number of social media companies committing to, and undertaking, actions to strengthen hate speech moderation approaches and engaged on their public policy for Sri Lanka influenced by national stakeholders (including CSOs) recommendations.
Indicator 1b: Number of newly tracked gender disaggregated indicators on social cohesion (defined by data generated under Outcome 1) by national partners.
Indicator 1c: Number, diversity & type of national stakeholders actively using data and evidence generated in practice, policies, and programming to address hate speech and support social cohesion efforts.
Outcome 2: National stakeholders actively engage in and lead early action to prevent and address hate speech and to support social cohesion
Indicator 2a: % and diversity of national key stakeholders targeted by the project reporting actively engaging in preventing and addressing hate speech and supporting social cohesion
Indicator 2c: % of sample stakeholders(disaggregated by gender, age, location etc.) demonstrate behavioral shifts in likelihood to share hate speech
Indicator 2c: # and types of platforms/ forums (including representing women’s and youth groups) engaged in inter-ethnic dialogue and exchanges on hate speech dynamics and trends and response of participants engaged

comment.narrative:
Outcome 1: Systematic research and evidence generation supporting the countering of online hate speech for effective advocacy and preventive action (UNDP) Indicator 1a: 1 (Facebook)
Indicator 1b: 0
Indicator 1c: 1 organization (very few organizations are currently using or have the technical capacities to use data to address hate speech and support social cohesion).
Outcome 2: National stakeholders actively engage in and lead early action to prevent and address hate speech and to support social cohesion Indicator 2a: TBD through scoping exercise
Indicator 2b: TBD
Indicator 2c: 3 (Inter-university platform, Youth Gaming Platform and CSOs working group – few such platforms currently exist and these are the most prominent/ relevant)
Not applicable since the project actively commences in January 2022.
Not applicable since the project actively commences in January 2022.

title.narrative: Sri Lanka: BTR1+ BTR2/NC4-Development of the First Biennial Transparency Report, the Second Biennial Transparency Report and Fourth National Communication of Sri Lanka to UNFCCC

description.narrative: The Project objective is to strengthen technical and institutional capacity to assist Sri Lanka to fulfill the climate change reporting commitments under the UNFCCC and Paris Agreement. As such, the Project will enable Sri Lanka to prepare and submit its First Biennial Transparency Report (BTR1), the Second Biennial Transparency Report (BTR2) and Fourth National Communication (NC4).

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

title.narrative: Integrated Social Protection Model for the New Poor

description.narrative: In the context of the severe economic crisis Sri Lanka is facing, social protection can offer immediate relief to households with lost or reduced income while building and strengthening the foundation for long-term inclusive growth. The proposal focuses on improving the identification of the newly poor not captured in existing social safety net databases, improving social protection data disaggregation and quality, integrating cash-based interventions with economic empowerment opportunities with a special focus on women and women headed households, adapting inclusive digital technologies to support effective, transparent, and efficient programme delivery and to reinforce broad-based resilience.

title.narrative: Design and Piloting of an Integrated Social Protection Model for the New Poor

title.narrative: Smallholder Farmer Resilience through Innovative Financing Project

description.narrative: This project aims to build on the existing work by UNDP in the agriculture sector and leverage the findingsfrom UNDP Sri Lanka SDG Investor Map, to implement an innovative agro-pastoral farming model, linkedto agriculture focused, inclusive financing products for 1000 women beneficiaries in the Northern SriLankan districts of Mullaitivu and Kilinochchi. The project will improve the productive capacity of womensmallholder farmers, improve access to finance and enhance community resilience and theresponsiveness of food systems. The program also aims to create pathways to sustain the initiativebeyond the project by building investor confidence through the creation of investible pipelines to mobilizeprivate capital for the development of food systems.

title.narrative: PIMS - 9623 - PPG - Promoting sustainable economic benefits through the conservation of critical biodiversity and ecosystem services in the Eastern Coastal Region of Sri Lanka

description.narrative: Promoting sustainable economic benefits through the conservation of critical biodiversity and ecosystem services in the Eastern coastal region of Sri Lanka. As described in the PIF, this project aims to address the current and future development pressures to the biologically rich terrestrial, coastal and marine ecosystems in the Southern and Eastern parts of the country through improved governance, policies and capacities of institutions for promotion of integrated landscape/seascape approaches to resource management; improved financing and incentives for nature-positive practices; demonstration of integrated ecosystem-based management and nature-based solutions; and replication/up-scaling. As per the project’s theory of change, this objective will be achieved through four components:

As described in the PIF, this project aims to address the current and future development pressures to the biologically rich terrestrial, coastal and marine ecosystems in the Southern and Eastern parts of the country through improved governance, policies and capacities of institutions for promotion of integrated landscape/seascape approaches to resource management; improved financing and incentives for nature-positive practices; demonstration of integrated ecosystem-based management and nature-based solutions; and replication/up-scaling.

title.narrative: JSB_Promoting Economic Governance through Anti-Corruption Policy Support

description.narrative: Sri Lanka’s ongoing socio-economic crisis has reversed development gains achieved and threatens further advancement. Deep rooted transparency and accountability gaps have been identified as a key contributor to the economic crisis of the country. Whilst progress has been made in enacting anti-corruption law and policy frameworks per the UN Convention against Corruption (ratified in 2005), major challenges remain in their implementation. Amongst the key challenges are a) lack of public trust in, capacity of, and resources for key anti-corruption institutions – resulting in unsuccessful prosecutions, and an increase in corruption in the absence of a deterrent; b) Inadequate legal frameworks, policies and regulations to cover emerging and sophisticated forms of corruption; c) Ineffective coordination among key institutions and stakeholders, including with the private sector and civil society; and d) Resistance to digitalization that could advance transparency and accountability in service delivery. The proposed

Socio-economic and political factors continue to negatively affect women of all ethnicities, religions and social classes preventing them from having control or influence over decisions that impact their businesses and large economic policies. This in turn leads women to be largely undervalued and unrecognised. The proposed project focuses on addressing gendered implications of the National Small to Medium Enterprise Policy in Sri Lanka through transforming structures and creating a favorable, equitable and accessible business environment for women in the micro, small and medium enterprise sector.

title.narrative: Enabling Environment for Electric Buses for Public Transport in Sri Lanka

description.narrative: Sri Lanka’s transport sector is the largest consumer of imported petroleum fuels, accounting for 78.4% of national fuel consumption in 2021. It also contributes significantly to green house gas emissions (GHG) responsible for 21% of the country’s total GHG emissions, with road transport alone accounting for three-quarters of transport-related emissions and 15% of total CO2 emissions.In 2023, Sri Lanka faced an unprecedented economic crisis, which led to severe energy security concerns. This crisis triggered fuel shortages, a quota-based fuel distribution system, and skyrocketing fuel prices, resulting in general inflation and a significant drop in fuel consumption, including for transport (a decline of approximately 50%). This crisis has underscored the urgent need for Sri Lanka to transition to low-carbon, green transport solutions. Such a transition is not only essential to reduce dependency on imported fuels but is also critical for fulfilling the country’s Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC) under international climate agreements.In response, this project proposes to establish a conducive policy, regulatory, and institutional framework for the promotion of electric bus mobility in public transport. Additionally, it aims to design a business model and financial incentive package to support the pilot the deployment of five 30–35-seater midi electric buses in the Colombo Metropolitan region which will serve as a catalyst a shift to low-carbon public transport in Sri Lanka.

The project contributes to UNSDCF Outcome 3/UNDP Outcome 2 by introducing and promoting efficient plastic waste management that will in turn contribute to enhancing community resilience to disaster risks caused by climate change. The project interventions will reduce plastic pollution and mitigates aggravation of disaster impacts (i.e., intensified floods in urban areas due to clogging of drainage systems), as well as reducing threats to environment such as biodiversity, especially fauna, contamination of environment with microplastics etc. By developing and implementing gender-sensitive, pro-poor strategies for plastic waste management, the project ensures that vulnerable communities benefit from strengthened livelihoods and increased incomes. The dissemination of lessons learned and best practices across all local authorities in Sri Lanka further supports sustainable natural resource management and biodiversity conservation. By sharing innovative practices, the project aligns with CPD Output 2.1 and 2.3, contributing to local level green growth while addressing the environmental, social, and economic dimensions of plastic waste management.

title.narrative: Accelerating the Just Transition of Sri Lanka’s Agriculture Sector Towards Net-Zero Pathways

description.narrative: Sri Lanka has been grappling with a multidimensional crisis, marked by economic instability, climate vulnerabilities, and socio-political challenges. The countrys recent economic downturn has exacerbated poverty levels, particularly among marginalized groups such as plantation workers and smallholder farmers. These communities, already facing historical inequities, are disproportionately affected by rising costs of living, lack of access to essential services, and the impacts of climate change. Strengthening the energy security, thereby the human security, of climate vulnerable communities, targeting women smallholder farmers, in the Kilinochchi and Nuwara Eliya districts, through the extension of viable, renewable energy-based transformative green technologies and modernization, integrating them into resilient agricultural supply and value chains, setting a foundation for a just transition clean energy and net-zero pathways.- Estimated Direct Beneficiaries: 2,024 smallholder farmers & estate sector community members (inclusive of beneficiaries’ household members, where 51% are women, and focusing on youth) smallholder farmers)- Estimated Indirect Beneficiaries: 4,500 dry zone & estate sector community members

description.narrative: Sri Lanka has been grappling with a multidimensional crisis, marked by economic instability, climate vulnerabilities, and socio-political challenges. The countrys recent economic downturn has exacerbated poverty levels, particularly among marginalized groups such as plantation workers and smallholder farmers. These communities, already facing historical inequities, are disproportionately affected by rising costs of living, lack of access to essential services, and the impacts of climate change.

A build-up of fiscal, macroeconomic and external imbalances and an unsustainable debt position culminated in an unprecedented economic crisis in Sri Lanka in 2022. Against the backdrop of dwindling concessional financing in the global fora, and Sri Lanka’s status as a lower middle-income country, access to financing is further limited. Sri Lanka’s external debt stood at 128% of its GDP at the end of 2022. Furthermore, UNDP data indicates that the highest contributor of Multidimensional Vulnerability is related to household debt-related vulnerabilities. Further, women and girls, in particular, bear the brunt of such disparities and suffer the most due to brunt of compounded discriminations resulting from crises, mainly due to entrenched gender roles, social norms and systemic barriers that limit their agency and access to resources, opportunities, and decision-making processes.This coupled with the country’s default status, existing fiscal constraints, and lack of proper budgetary allocations for sustainable development are continuing to be limiting factors, making investments toward sustainable development even more difficult. Owing to the compounding nature and differentiated impacts particularly on women, enhancing women’s access to financing also remains a priority. Against this backdrop, there are opportunities to put in place certain foundations to unlock sustainable financing. UNDP through the innovative financing portfolio is undertaking a two-fold approach to our financing interventions. This largely consists of planning – setting up the groundwork and the foundations for financing frameworks, and financing instruments, which collectively aim to promote an integrated approach to sustainable/innovating financing for sustainable growth (taking into account both public & private; domestic and foreign investments).

title.narrative: Number of regional, national and sub-national initiatives, policies, and strategies, that with UNDP support protect and promote: a) Civil society to function in the public sphere and contribute to sustainable development b) Inclusive spaces, mechanisms and capacities for public dialogue c) Access to reliable information on issues of public concern

description.narrative:
a) This sub-indicator aims to measure UNDP support to strengthening the enabling environment for civil society actors to function as well as civil society actors’ ability to participate in public dialogues and public decision making processes. Such support includes a focus on one or more of the following: * Legislative or regulatory measures that advance the realization of fundamental freedoms related to civic space, especially freedom of expression (ICCPR Art. 19); freedom of peaceful assembly (ICCPR Art. 21); freedom of association (ICCPR Art. 22) * Removing barriers preventing the active and meaningful participation of specific population groups in development, with an emphasis on women, and women organizations, movements and networks; young people, and youth organizations, movements and networks; and other historically marginalized population groups and their organizations. Barriers may include capacities (technical, financial, economic) as well as social norms.
b) This sub-indicator aims to measure UNDP support to spaces, mechanisms and capacities for public dialogue. This includes: * Promotion and protection of physical and online spaces for civic engagement and multistakeholder dialogue * Mechanisms for public input (virtual and physical) into policy-making such as public meetings; advisory committees; multi-stakeholder policy groups; community forums; spaces created by civil society * Mechanisms to promote social accountability in the context of service delivery such as, for example, mechanisms to gather citizen feedback on public administration performance or enable different types of participatory audits.
c) This sub-indicator aims to measure UNDP support to people’s access to reliable information. Such support includes a focus on one or more of the following: Constitutional, statutory and/or policy guarantees for public access to information and strengthening information integrity * Strengthened capacities of public institutions to provide comprehensible, accurate and timely information on issues of public concern; * Measures and initiatives focused on strengthening integrity of information ecosystems and addressing information pollution * Measures and initiatives focused on strengthening independent media, including community media, public broadcasters, and online media.

title.narrative: To what extent the country's institutions, systems, or stakeholders have capacities to support fulfilment of nationally and internationally ratified human rights obligations: a) Rule of law and justice b) Human rights c) Private sector, including publicly owned companies

description.narrative:
This indicator is an ordinal indicator that illustrates the number of countries where UNDP is supporting with institutions, systems, or stakeholders with capacities to support fulfilment of nationally and internationally ratified human rights obligations: a) Rule of law and justice b) Human rights c) Private sector, including publicly owned companies
In order to collect data for this indicator, the Country Office should identify whether the above (a-c) is “0 = Not in place”, “1 = Capacity low”, “2 = Capacity neither low nor high”, “3 = Capacity high” or “4 = Capacity very high”. If “3 = Capacity high” or “4 = Capacity very high”, it counts as one of the countries where the institutions and/or systems have capacities supporting the fulfillment of nationally and internationally ratified human rights obligations.
Rule of law and justice: As articulated in the ‘Declaration of the High-level Meeting of the UN General Assembly on the Rule of Law at the National and International Levels,’ (A/RES/67/1) which states, inter alia, that ‘….We recognize the importance of national ownership in rule of law activities, strengthening justice and security institutions that are accessible and responsive to the needs and rights of all individuals and which build trust and promote social cohesion and economic prosperity’ (paragraph 11).
Human rights: as per inter-governmentally agreed norms and standards (e.g. the Paris Principles, and UN General Assembly Resolution A/RES/48/134 of December 1993).

title.narrative: Number of new measures that improved agility and responsiveness of local governance institutions for: a) Basic service delivery, b) Prevention

description.narrative: This indicator tracks UNDP support to putting in place new measures that have improved agility and responsiveness of local governance, defined as the combined set of institutions, systems and processes at the subnational level through which services, including security and welfare, are provided and through which the population articulate their interests and needs, mediate their differences and exercise their rights and obligations. A key part of these systems is the capacity of subnational governments and non-governmental organizations, in terms of agility and responsiveness, to ensure the delivery of services in a way that strengthens the social contract. For this purpose, what matters in not only what services are delivered, but crucially how they are delivered. Examples of the measures that may promote inclusive service delivery through agility and an increased responsiveness include partnership mechanisms developed to involve more stakeholders; participatory systems set up; service design improvements; training and/or capacity development of staff; development of open data sources; mechanisms for civic engagement set up; enabling further civic space; better communication systems; enhanced complaint mechanisms and management of grievances; new transparency mechanisms such as open budgets or anti-corruption measures; and collaborative networks across the public and private sector, among others. The aim of these measures is to improve quantity, access and quality of service delivery and prevention, by expanding participation, inclusivity, equal access for all, and alignment with demand and expectations of the population.

title.narrative: Number of partnerships with women-led civil society organizations and other bodies and networks to advance women’s leadership and participation and gender equality

description.narrative: This indicator measures the number of partnerships with women-led civil society organizations, and other bodies and networks that work to advance women’s leadership and participation, as well as gender equality. They are measured through signed agreements where they exist or other forms of collaboration such as joint activities or reports. These partnerships can entail joint efforts to promote legislation and mechanisms to advance gender equality, eliminate discriminatory laws, end gender-based violence, address climate change, and advance women’s leadership and equal participation in decision-making. This indicator will provide evidence on UNDP’s collaboration with women’s organizations as important levers for advancing gender equality and promoting women’s agency, rights, and equal participation, while allowing for taking necessary interventions to support these organizations and networks.

title.narrative: Strengthening The Measuring and Monitoring of SDG 16 In Sri Lanka

description.narrative: The project will support the implementation of the SDG16 Survey, an international survey instrument jointly developed by UNDP, UNODC and OHCHR in collaboration with member states, that countries can use to measure progress on many of the survey-based indicators under SDG16. The initiative will support Sri Lanka to implement specific survey modules based on national needs and priorities which can be attached to household surveys primarily conducted by Department of Censors and Statistics (DCS).The initiative will provide a more comprehensive and accurate national and global picture of progress towards SDG 16 on peace, justice and strong institutions and strengthen the evidence base for UNDP governance programming.

title.narrative: Blue Economy Pipeline Development with a focus on Marine Tourism

description.narrative: Sri Lanka’s marine tourism industry can evolve and unlock further opportunities for economic development and creation of ‘blue jobs’. By capitalizing on its natural resources, Sri Lanka can further drive economic growth and ensure the preservation of its marine environments for future generations. Against this backdrop, there are opportunities to put in place certain foundations to develop a pipeline of bankable projects focused on Sri Lanka’s blue economy. In this regard, UNDP Sri Lanka’s integrated service offer on blue tourism, as part of this project, consists of 1) a blue economy development approach with a Marine Spatial Plan as the central governance framework, 2) collaboration with partners such as the Asian Development Bank (ADB) on marine tourism business models, 3) Northern and Eastern province tourism development, and other activities that are central to innovation in blue economy for sustainable growth.

title.narrative: Number of trained targeted, youth, children and journalists, confirmed their ability to combat corruption.

description.narrative: The indicator "Number of trained targeted youth, children, and journalists confirmed their ability to combat corruption" refers to the total count of individuals from these specific groups who, after receiving training, have demonstrated or acknowledged their capacity to actively engage in anti-corruption efforts. This confirmation may be based on post-training assessments, self-evaluations, certifications, or practical demonstrations of knowledge and skills. The indicator reflects the effectiveness of training programs in empowering key stakeholders to recognize, report, and prevent corruption within their communities or professional roles.

title.narrative: Scale-up and Sustain the Low Carbon Municipal Solid Waste Management

description.narrative: The project presents an opportunity to consolidate past efforts, scale up proven practices, and introduce sustainable, system-wide approaches that go beyond isolated interventions, ensuring long-term environmental, social, and economic benefits. The project interventions introduce integrated MSWM solutions that address the entire chain from waste generation to final disposal. The objectives of this project are to: 1. Review, strengthen and develop inclusive policy, regulatory, institutional, and green financing frameworks on municipal solid waste management (MSWM) at provincial and local levels. 2. Demonstrate viable, sustainable Public Private Partnership (PPP) options for total MSWM solutions and a system for GHG emission reduction management in the selected Local Authorities (LAs). 3. Gather and share knowledge, support training, replication, and scalability of project results, and manage, monitor, and evaluate the project performance including GHG emission reduction.

title.narrative: Support to the National Digital Economy Blueprint

description.narrative: The IP will be implemented through UNDP’s Direct Implementation Modality, whereby UNDP will utilize its technical and administrative capacity to assume the responsibility for mobilizing and effectively implementing the activities to reach the expected outputs. UNDP assumes overall management responsibility and accountability. As per UNDP’s Financial Regulations and Rules (FRR) (21.02), the following general principles will be given due consideration; Best value for money; Inclusivity, Fairness, Integrity and Transparency. The IP will be managed by the Digital and Service Design Officer at Citra Social Innovation Lab, a joint initiative between UNDP Sri Lanka and the Government of Sri Lanka, currently anchored at the Prime Minister’s Office. The Digital and Service Design Officer and the Citra team will assume responsibility for: • Managing the overall conduct of the project • Implementing activities by mobilizing goods and services • Checking on progress and plan deviations Ensuring that changes are controlled and problems addressed • Monitoring progress and risks • Reporting on progress including measures to address challenges and opportunities.

title.narrative: Towards a more impactful and scalable Government-led Empowerment Program in Sri Lanka

description.narrative: In 2025, the newly elected Government of Sri Lanka launched the Empowerment Program, co-financed by the World Bank Social Protection Project. The Program aims to improve the livelihoods and welfare of vulnerable households currently receiving social assistance and working informally, through holistic interventions that include upskilling, psychosocial support, business grants, and continuous coaching. This project supports the government in tackling these challenges by assessing and testing three solutions that could catalyze impact and scale:1. Assessing and piloting more accessible formal financing – through Samurdhi Banks - that would allow Program beneficiaries to grow and face shocks.2. Assessing viable business models to encourage the care economy by activating and collaborating with community-based organizations (CBOs) in rural areas.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

description.narrative: Cyclone Ditwah has rapidly evolved into one of Sri Lanka’s most devastating flood disasters in two decades, with new information revealing a deepening humanitarian and early recovery crisis. Since making landfall on 28 November, the cyclone has affected over one million people (455,405 families) across all 25 districts, with 639 lives lost and 203 still missing. Over 180,000 people remain displaced in 1,094 government-run safety centres, while access to affected communities continues to be disrupted by persistent flooding. The heaviest impacts are concentrated in Gampaha, Colombo, Puttalam, Mannar, Trincomalee and Batticaloa, with severe landslides in Kandy, Badulla and Matale causing extensive loss of life and long-term livelihood damage. More than 15,000 homes have been destroyed, and widespread damage to roads, bridges, rail lines, and power infrastructure, including a flooded substation, continues to hinder mobility, communications, and relief operations—particularly around the Kelani River basin.The initiation plan is to support early recovery sector related activities in support of national partner capacity strengthening, cash for work and other mechanisms to build back better. UNDP’s key interventions in this sector include Debris management, Livelihoods recovery, Community Infrastructure Rehabilitation, Community Security, Restoring Local Governance Functions, Advancing Gender Equality, Energy Services in Crisis, and Health Services in Crisis.

title.narrative: Governance, Rule of Law and Peacebuilding for Sustainable Development

description.narrative: The second output of the project will focus on strengthening the capacities of state and non-state institutions to respond to gendered hate speech, dis/misinformation/malinformation, and TFGBV by establishing cross-ministerial coordination and protocols such as mapping mandates and workflows across the Ministries of Health & Mass Media, Women & Child Affairs, and Digital Economy, and co-creating role clarity, referral SOPs, escalation pathways, joint communications, and an action calendar, to convert fragmented efforts into survivor-centred, coherent responses; developing and maintaining a consolidated knowledge repository that curates prior and ongoing UNDP products and adds Sri Lanka-specific guidance, templates, and measurement tools—to standardize and accelerate good practice; and initiating a national media awareness campaign—co-designing and pre-testing messaging with media partners, women leaders, and human rights defenders to counter harmful narratives, signpost services, and monitor reach and sentiment—to shift norms and increase help-seeking and institutional responsiveness

title.narrative: Net-Zero, Nature-Positive Dairy: Climate-Smart Dairy Value Chains through Energy Transition & Nature-Based Solutions

description.narrative: Sri Lanka’s dairy sector is at a critical juncture, facing cascading climate shocks, economic hardship, and deepening rural inequality—challenges that disproportionately affect women, youth, and marginalized estate communities. Rural poverty has more than doubled since 2019, and women—who make up a significant share of the agricultural workforce—are especially vulnerable due to limited access to resources, technology, and decision-making platforms. In climate-vulnerable districts like Kilinochchi and Nuwara Eliya, smallholder dairy farmers, including women-headed households and aspiring youth agri-entrepreneurs, struggle with declining productivity, input shortages, and nutrition insecurity. The destabilization of the dairy sector threatens household food security, particularly for children and women in estate areas where dairy is often the only accessible protein source. By establishing a Climate-Smart Dairy ecosystem, the intervention will reduce emissions, enhance dairy yields, and build adaptive capacity, supporting a just and inclusive transition toward a sustainable, climate-resilient dairy sector in Sri Lanka.Direct beneficiaries (number, men/women): 750 smallholder famers (400 women/350 men) and 100 government officers.Indirect Beneficiaries: 5,000 (including family members, aspiring youth agri-entrepreneurs, veterinary officers, animal health professionals, local milk collectors & other community members)
